title: Pressing and drying handmade paper sheets
subtitle: The post-forming steps that determine flatness, surface texture, and weight
excerpt: Pulling a sheet is only the first step. What happens next: couching onto felt, pressing out water, and drying flat or on a board, determines whether the finished sheet is smooth or textured, stiff or limp, and whether it will buckle the first time it takes ink.
sourceNotes: The couching, pressing, and drying methods described here follow the traditional Western sheet-forming process documented in the pre-1928 papermaking literature. Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), describes the pressing and drying stages. Internet Archive copy available.

glossaryTerms.definition: The action of transferring a freshly formed wet sheet from the papermaking mould to a couching cloth by pressing the mould face-down onto the cloth.
A stack of alternate wet sheets and couching cloths built up for pressing. Pressing a post expels water from all sheets simultaneously.

content.text: The traditional papermaking sequence has three stages after the sheet is pulled:

(transferring the wet sheet from the mould to a cloth), pressing (squeezing out water), and drying (removing the remaining moisture). Each stage affects the final character of the sheet. Pressing determines thickness and compaction; the drying method determines surface texture and flatness.

items.name: Couching cloths (wool felt, kitchen J-cloth, or tight-weave linen)
items.qty: At least 2 per sheet, plus spares for the post

items.name: Press boards (two pieces of 8 mm plywood, at least 30 mm larger on all sides than the sheets)
Heavy weight (at least 15 kg, e.g. a stack of books or concrete blocks)
Papermaking sponge (for blotting the mould before couching)
Flat drying board (melamine or smooth plywood)

attrs.heading: Electrical safety
attrs.body: Papermaking involves water and, if using a powered blender for pulp, electricity. Keep the blender unplugged whenever adding or removing fibre. Keep all electrical equipment away from the wet vat.

content.text: Once the sheet has formed on the mould surface and most of the free water has drained away, tilt the mould at 45 degrees and press the papermaking sponge gently against the back (mesh side) of the mould to draw away any surface water pooled at the deckle edge.

Lay a couching cloth flat on the press board. Hold the mould with the sheet face-down above the cloth. In one continuous rolling movement, press the near edge of the mould down onto the cloth first, then roll the mould across so the sheet lies flat on the cloth. Do not drag the mould sideways; the rolling action releases the sheet cleanly.

Press firmly on the back of the mould for a moment, then lift it away from one corner. The sheet should remain on the couching cloth. If the sheet lifts with the mould, the fibres were too dilute or the mould was not rolled smoothly.
Lay another couching cloth on top of the couched sheet. Pull and couch another sheet on top of this cloth. Repeat until you have all the sheets you intend to press in a single
. The stack should be: board, cloth, sheet, cloth, sheet, cloth..., board.

Set the second press board on top of the post. Apply the weight evenly across the board. Press for at least 20 minutes; water will pool around the edges. Replace the wet couching cloths with dry ones and press for another 20 minutes for a more thoroughly pressed sheet.

Free drying: lift each sheet off its couching cloth and hang it over a wooden drying line or lay it on a wire drying rack. The sheet dries freely and will cockle (form gentle undulations). Cockled sheets have a soft, natural texture and are often preferred for watercolour work.

Board drying: brush the wet sheet directly onto a smooth melamine board or glass plate using a few strokes of clean water to adhere it. The sheet dries under tension and releases flat with a smooth surface on the board side. Board-dried sheets are better for calligraphy and pen work.

Weighted drying: place the sheet between two couching cloths and leave it under the press boards with a reduced weight. The sheet dries flat and even, with a slight texture from the couching cloth impressed on both faces. Good for bookbinding paper.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that appear after pressing and drying.

items.symptom: Sheet sticks to the couching cloth and tears when lifted
items.cause: Fibres were too short or the sheet was too thin (insufficient pulp concentration in the vat).
items.fix: Add more pulp to the vat before the next pull. Use couching cloths that are lint-free; a cloth with loose fibres is more likely to bind to the sheet.

items.symptom: Dried sheet is brittle and breaks on folding
items.cause: Paper dried too quickly under high heat, or fibre not prepared correctly.
items.fix: Dry at room temperature, not in direct heat. If using cotton linter, ensure the pulp was beaten long enough for the fibres to mat properly.
